content.assessment: The Finnish lens is skeptical of admission-mechanism tweaks that leave the tracking age and structure intact. Finland's own experience shows that even in a nominally comprehensive, untracked system, informal selection mechanisms ('emphasized classes') reasserted sorting by achievement from the 1990s onward, and school-choice-driven segregation has grown despite the absence of formal tracks. This suggests that redesigning admission criteria for an early (age 10-12) selective track, without addressing the tracking age itself, is unlikely to durably reduce sorting, since coaching and advantage will migrate to whatever screening instrument remains, mirroring how Finnish 'emphasized classes' substituted formal untracked structure with informal testing-based selection.
opportunities: Could serve as a transitional or complementary measure alongside structural reform, similar to how Finland paired delay with curriculum/teacher-training bundling (F-live-finnish_reform-06)
strengths: Targets an identifiable, documented exam-coaching market mechanism analogous to the informal testing Finland later saw emerge inside its own comprehensive system (F-live-finnish_reform-08)
Uses existing regulatory levers (interview cap) rather than requiring new legal architecture, a lower-durability-risk approach consistent with incremental adjustment
threats: Finland's 'emphasized classes' precedent shows that determined higher-SES demand for selection finds new informal channels even under strict formal rules, suggesting coaching-resistant redesign risks the same substitution effect within Hungary's still-tracked structure (F-live-finnish_reform-08)

Finland's recent PISA decline and growing SES gaps show that even successful comprehensive systems face erosion pressure; a much weaker admission-only reform inside a still-tracked system offers a materially weaker equity guarantee (F-live-finnish_reform-07)
weaknesses: Leaves the early tracking age (10-12) untouched, which cross-national evidence associates with higher inequality regardless of admission-instrument design (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
Does not address the comprehensive-provision question at all; Finland's gains came from eliminating the dual-track structure, not from refining entry criteria within it (F-live-finnish_reform-06)
